message.content:
Test file: src/game-of-life.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/game-of-life.ts
Passing tests: 6
Recent Green phase: No code change — reproduction test already satisfied.

Refactor the implementation while keeping all tests green.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, return when done.

attachment.content:
- green: TDD Green Phase
- red: TDD Red Phase
- test-list: TDD Test List Phase
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

input.command: pnpm exec eslint src/ --format json 2>&1 | tail -200

content.text:
All tests pass (6 passing, 4 todo). ESLint reports zero smells.

## PRE Measurements

### ESLint Smells (PRE)
- **Total smells**: 0 across the entire src/ directory
- **Cognitive complexity violations**: 0
- All ESLint rules clean

### Naming Evaluation
- `cellKey([x, y])` → encodes a cell as a string key. Name reveals intent.
- `parseCellKey(k)` → reverse of `cellKey`. Symmetrical, intent clear.
- `NEIGHBOR_OFFSETS` → the 8 surrounding deltas. Clear.
- `countLiveNeighbors(cells)` → tallies how many live neighbours each cell-position has. Name is accurate (it counts live neighbours, keyed by position).
- `survives(neighborCount, isAlive)` → returns whether a cell at a position will be alive in the next generation. The name is slightly misleading because it covers both "live cell survives" (Rule 2) and "dead cell is born" (Rule 4). A more honest name could be `isAliveNext` or `willBeAlive`.
- `nextGeneration(cells)` → top-level. Clear.

**Decision**: The `survives` name applies to both survival and birth — slightly stretched. Candidate for rename, but not the worst issue.

### APP Mass (PRE)

**`cellKey`** (1 line):
- Bindings: 2 (x, y); Invocations: 1 (template literal effectively one interpolation, counted as 1)
- Mass: 2·1 + 1·2 = 4

**`parseCellKey`**:
- Bindings: 3 (k, xs, ys); Invocations: 3 (split, Number, Number); Constants: 1 (","); 
- Mass: 3·1 + 3·2 + 1·1 = 10

**`countLiveNeighbors`**:
- Constants: 2 (0, the initial); Bindings: 6 (cells, counts, x, y, dx, dy, k → ~7); Invocations: ~5 (Map ctor, cellKey, counts.set, counts.get, ??); Loops: 2 (two `for...of`); Assignments: 0 (uses .set, an invocation, no `=` mutation)
- Mass ≈ 2·1 + 7·1 + 5·2 + 0·4 + 2·5 + 0·6 = 29

**`survives`**:
- Bindings: 2 (neighborCount, isAlive); Invocations: 0; Conditionals: 0 (it's a pure boolean expression, no `if`/`?:` but it has 2 `===` comparisons + 1 `||` + 1 `&&`)
- Treating logical operators as conditional-like: ~2 conditionals from `||`/`&&`. Mass ≈ 2·1 + 2·4 = 10. Treating as pure expression: ~5.

**`nextGeneration`**:
- Bindings: ~5 (cells, alive, neighborCounts, next, k, count); Invocations: ~5 (Set, map, cellKey, countLiveNeighbors, survives, alive.has, next.push, parseCellKey) ≈ 8; Conditionals: 1 (if); Loops: 1 (for...of); Constants: 0
- Mass ≈ 5·1 + 8·2 + 1·4 + 1·5 = 30

### McCabe Cyclomatic Complexity (PRE)
- `cellKey`: 1
- `parseCellKey`: 1
- `countLiveNeighbors`: start 1 + 2 for-loops + 1 for `??` = **4**
- `survives`: start 1 + `||` + `&&` = **3**
- `nextGeneration`: start 1 + for-loop + if = **3**

### Worst-case target

`countLiveNeighbors` has the highest McCabe (4) and the highest APP mass (29). It's a good refactoring candidate. Looking at it:

```typescript
const countLiveNeighbors = (cells: Cell[]): Map<string, number> => {
  const counts = new Map<string, number>();
  for (const [x, y] of cells) {
    for (const [dx, dy] of NEIGHBOR_OFFSETS) {
      const k = cellKey([x + dx, y + dy]);
      counts.set(k, (counts.get(k) ?? 0) + 1);
    }
  }
  return counts;
};
```

The two nested `for...of` loops both contribute to McCabe and cognitive load. I can replace them with a `flatMap` + `reduce`:

```typescript
const countLiveNeighbors = (cells: Cell[]): Map<string, number> =>
  cells
    .flatMap(([x, y]) => NEIGHBOR_OFFSETS.map(([dx, dy]) => cellKey([x + dx, y + dy])))
    .reduce((counts, k) => counts.set(k, (counts.get(k) ?? 0) + 1), new Map<string, number>());
```

But this trades clarity for a one-liner. The current imperative version is more readable. Let me think differently.

A cleaner angle: the inner `counts.set(k, (counts.get(k) ?? 0) + 1)` pattern is duplicated logic (the increment-or-init pattern). I can extract that, but it doesn't reduce McCabe much.

Let me look at the **whole file** for genuinely worthwhile improvements:

1. `survives` has a stretched name — Rule 2 (clarity) is the highest-priority rule. Renaming to `isAliveNext` makes intent clearer because it covers both birth (3 neighbours) and survival (2 neighbours + currently alive).

2. The two `===` constants `NEIGHBORS_TO_SURVIVE = 2` and `NEIGHBORS_TO_BIRTH = 3` are named well.

3. The `cellKey`/`parseCellKey` round-tripping through strings is a known "stringly typed" approach. But changing it would be a larger refactor.

Let me apply the **naming refactor** to `survives` since it's the clarity win that Rule 2 prioritizes. This is a small, isolated change.

Actually, wait — let me reconsider. The function `survives` returns `true` for both Rule 2 (live cell with 2 or 3 neighbours stays alive) and Rule 4 (dead cell with exactly 3 neighbours becomes alive). The name `survives` is misleading for Rule 4 — a dead cell can't "survive", it's born. Let me rename to `isAliveNext`.

input.command: pnpm exec eslint src/ --format json 2>&1 | tail -200

content.text:
All 6 tests still pass and ESLint stays clean.

## POST Measurements

### ESLint Smells (POST)
- **Total smells**: 0 (unchanged)
- All clean

### APP Mass (POST) for `isAliveNext`
- Identical structure to `survives`; only the identifier changed.
- Mass: ~10 (unchanged)

### McCabe (POST) for `isAliveNext`
- start 1 + `||` + `&&` = **3** (unchanged)

### Pre/Post Deltas

| Metric | PRE | POST | Delta |
|---|---|---|---|
| ESLint smells | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| Cognitive complexity (target) | 0 reported | 0 reported | 0 |
| APP mass (`survives`/`isAliveNext`) | ~10 | ~10 | 0 |
| McCabe (`survives`/`isAliveNext`) | 3 | 3 | 0 |

The structural metrics didn't change — this was a pure Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) refactoring. The new name `isAliveNext` truthfully covers both the "live cell with 2 or 3 neighbours survives" path AND the "dead cell with exactly 3 neighbours becomes alive" path. The old name `survives` implied the cell was already alive, which only fits half the logic the function expresses.

Per the workflow's own rule: "Rule 2 trumps APP" — clarity wins even when other metrics are flat.

### Why I didn't pursue `countLiveNeighbors`
It had the highest pre-McCabe (4) and highest mass (29), but a candidate flatMap/reduce rewrite traded the two clear nested loops for a less-readable functional chain — that would have raised invocation cost and lowered clarity (a Rule 2 regression) without removing branches at the underlying level. The imperative form is the clearer expression of "for every live cell, bump each neighbour's counter". I considered it and judged the change would worsen at least one metric (clarity) without a meaningful gain.

## Tests
6/6 passing, 4 todo remaining.

## Summary

Refactoring Complete:
- **Refactoring**: Renamed `survives` → `isAliveNext` to honestly cover both survival (Rule 2) and birth (Rule 4) of cells
- **Smell delta**: 0 → 0
- **Cognitive delta** (target): 0 → 0 (no SonarJS report — function below threshold both before and after)
- **APP mass delta** (target): ~10 → ~10
- **McCabe delta** (target): 3 → 3
- **Tests**: 6 passing, 4 todo — all green

Relevant files:
- `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-30_16-52-13_game-of-life-example-mapping_v6.4-metric-driven-refactor_opus-4-7-no-thinking/src/game-of-life.ts`
- `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-30_16-52-13_game-of-life-example-mapping_v6.4-metric-driven-refactor_opus-4-7-no-thinking/src/game-of-life.spec.ts`
